thread.title: High-profile Oklahoma attorney and judge remains in jail after outburst
thread.title_full: High-profile Oklahoma attorney and judge remains in jail after outburst

author: Meghan Mosley
title: High-profile Oklahoma attorney and judge remains in jail after outburst
text:
ZIP Advertisement High-profile Oklahoma attorney and judge remains in jail after outburst A well-known attorney and judge was jailed after a judge held him in contempt of court on Thursday.
Share Copy Link Copy {copyShortcut} to copy Link copied! Updated: 5:18 PM CST Nov 22, 2024 Meghan Mosley Reporter High-profile Oklahoma attorney and judge remains in jail after outburst A well-known attorney and judge was jailed after a judge held him in contempt of court on Thursday.
Share Copy Link Copy {copyShortcut} to copy Link copied! Updated: 5:18 PM CST Nov 22, 2024 TODAY. THE JUDGE DID NOT BUY IT. YEAH, EVAN. THAT’S RIGHT. KELLY LYNN IS TYPICALLY IN THE COURTROOM WITH A BRIEFCASE. BUT TODAY HE APPEARED WITH HANDCUFFS AND A JUMPSUIT. ULTIMATELY, THE JUDGE TOLD HIM HE HAS TO STAY IN JAIL. DO YOU HAVE ANY COMMENT TODAY, MR. LYNN? NAH, NOT AT THIS TIME. NOT THIS TIME. YOU’LL HEAR FROM US. THAT WAS KELLY LYNN LEAVING THE COURTROOM IN A DIFFERENT OUTFIT THAN HE’S USED TO IN A JUMPSUIT. AND CUFFS. LYNN WAS HELD IN CONTEMPT THURSDAY FOLLOWING WHAT’S BEEN DESCRIBED AS AN OUTBURST AND AGGRESSIVE DISRUPTION TOWARD A PUBLIC DEFENDER INSIDE OF AN OKLAHOMA COUNTY COURTROOM. YOU MIGHT REMEMBER HIS NAME BECAUSE HE USED TO SERVE ON NORMAN CITY COUNCIL. HE WAS ORDERED TO SERVE FIVE DAYS IN JAIL FOR DISORDERLY CONDUCT AND DISRUPTIVE BEHAVIOR. THE PRESIDING ADMINISTRATIVE DISTRICT JUDGE, RICHARD OGDEN, TOLD ME LYNN BECAME, QUOTE, BELLIGERENT, ANGRY AND UPSET IN THE COURTROOM, HE SAYS, RARELY, IF EVER, DO THEY CITE ATTORNEYS FOR CONTEMPT. HE SAYS THIS CONDUCT IS EXTREMELY UNCOMMON AND DOESN’T HAPPEN OFTEN. TODAY IN THE COURTROOM, LYNN TOLD A JUDGE HE KNEW HIS BEHAVIOR WAS OUT OF LINE AND UNACCEPTABLE, BUT HE WANTED TO SPEND THIS WEEKEND WITH HIS FAMILY FOR HIS KIDS BIRTHDAY. ULTIMATELY, JUDGE STINSON ORDERED HIM TO BE RELEASED MONDAY AT 12, WHICH IS ONE LESS DAY THAN HIS ORIGINAL SENTENCE. ANY COMMENT TODAY, MR. LYNN AND THE JUDGE ALSO SAID SHE DIDN’T ORDER HIM TO SPEND TEN DAYS IN JAIL BECAUSE SHE KNEW THAT WOULD FALL UNDER THANKSGIVING. REPORTING LI GET LOCAL BREAKING NEWS ALERTS The latest breaking updates, delivered straight to your email inbox.

Updated: 5:18 PM CST Nov 22, 2024 Meghan Mosley Reporter A well-known attorney and judge was jailed after a judge held him in contempt of court on Thursday.Get the latest news stories of interest by clicking here.Kelly Lynn asked a judge to let him out of the rare contempt charge Friday afternoon, but the judge didn’t budge.Lynn can typically be found inside a courtroom with a briefcase, but on Friday, he appeared in a jumpsuit and was handcuffed. The judge ultimately ordered him to remain in jail.“Nah, not at this time. You’ll hear from us soon, though,” Lynn said to KOCO 5 Friday as he was leaving a courtroom.Lynn was held in contempt of court on Thursday following what’s been described as an outburst and aggressive disruption toward a public defender inside of an Oklahoma County courtroom.You might remember his name, Lynn, who used to serve on the Norman City Council. He was ordered to serve five days in jail for disorderly conduct and disruptive behavior.The presiding Administrative District Judge Richard Ogden told KOCO 5 that Lynn became “belligerent, angry and upset in the courtroom.”>> Download the KOCO 5 AppOgden said rarely, if ever, do judges cite attorneys for contempt. He said Lynn’s conduct is extremely uncommon.On Friday, Lynn told a judge he knew his behavior was out of line and unacceptable, but he wanted to spend the weekend with his family for his child’s birthday.The judge ordered Lynn to be released on Nov. 25 at noon, which is one day less than his original sentence.The judge said she didn’t order him to serve 10 days in jail because she knew that would leave him behind bars on Thanksgiving.Top Headlines‘Together forever’: Couple going through cancer diagnosis receives dream weddingDocuments allege Oklahoma church coverup of man accused of sexually assaulting childrenStillwater man accused of sexually assaulting unconscious victims recovering from dental surgeryOKC crews continue cleanup after crash causes hazardous material spill off I-40Double murder is small Oklahoma town may be tip of the iceberg of large crime ring OKLAHOMA CITY — A well-known attorney and judge was jailed after a judge held him in contempt of court on Thursday.
Get the latest news stories of interest by clicking here.
Advertisement Kelly Lynn asked a judge to let him out of the rare contempt charge Friday afternoon, but the judge didn’t budge.
Lynn can typically be found inside a courtroom with a briefcase, but on Friday, he appeared in a jumpsuit and was handcuffed. The judge ultimately ordered him to remain in jail.
“Nah, not at this time. You’ll hear from us soon, though,” Lynn said to KOCO 5 Friday as he was leaving a courtroom.
Lynn was held in contempt of court on Thursday following what’s been described as an outburst and aggressive disruption toward a public defender inside of an Oklahoma County courtroom.
You might remember his name, Lynn, who used to serve on the Norman City Council.
He was ordered to serve five days in jail for disorderly conduct and disruptive behavior.
The presiding Administrative District Judge Richard Ogden told KOCO 5 that Lynn became “belligerent, angry and upset in the courtroom.”
>> Download the KOCO 5 App
Ogden said rarely, if ever, do judges cite attorneys for contempt. He said Lynn’s conduct is extremely uncommon.
On Friday, Lynn told a judge he knew his behavior was out of line and unacceptable, but he wanted to spend the weekend with his family for his child’s birthday.
The judge ordered Lynn to be released on Nov. 25 at noon, which is one day less than his original sentence.
The judge said she didn’t order him to serve 10 days in jail because she knew that would leave him behind bars on Thanksgiving.
